g.data-narration: Welcome to Virtualization! Today we explore five implementation levels from hardware to applications.
Virtualization enables multiple virtual environments to run on single physical hardware, maximizing resource utilization.
g.text: Technology that creates virtual instances of computing resources - hardware platforms, storage devices,
or network resources. Enables efficient resource sharing and isolation.

g.data-narration: Virtualization operates at five distinct levels - from ISA instruction emulation to application runtime environments.
Level 1 (ISA): Emulates one instruction set on another using interpretation or binary translation. QEMU and Bochs are examples.
g.text: ★ Emulates one ISA using another through interpretation or binary translation

g.data-narration: Level 2 (HAL): Hypervisors create virtual hardware for guest OS. Supports full, para, and hardware-assisted virtualization.
g.text: ★ Hypervisor creates virtual hardware interfaces; guest OS runs directly on virtualized hardware

g.data-narration: Level 3 (OS): Containers share host kernel but have isolated file systems and processes. Docker and LXC are popular examples.
g.text: ★ Single OS kernel supports multiple isolated containers; lightweight and fast sharing host kernel

g.data-narration: Level 4: Library/API level intercepts system calls and redirects them. Wine runs Windows apps on Linux by translating API calls.
g.text: ★ Intercepts and redirects API/library calls; allows apps to run on non-native platforms

g.data-narration: Level 5: Application/Process level provides virtual runtime environments. JVM and .NET CLR execute bytecode, achieving write-once-run-anywhere.
g.text: ★ Virtual runtime environment executes bytecode; JIT compilation provides near-native performance

g.data-narration: This comparison shows trade-offs: ISA level offers maximum flexibility but lowest performance; OS level offers best performance-to-isolation ratio.
Virtualization levels map to cloud services: HAL level provides IaaS VMs, OS level enables containers for PaaS, Application level offers serverless.
Remember the five levels with mnemonic: I Have Olive-Leaf Arrangements. Key: ISA=flexible/slow, OS=fast/containers, HAL=cloud IaaS.
g.text: • ISA level = highest flexibility, lowest performance
• OS containers share kernel; VMs have own kernel
